name_english: Fatima, known as Sutayta (also called Najiya)
father: Judge Kamal al-Din Mahmud ibn Shirayin al-Hanafi
description_en: Fatima (called Sutayta) was a Cairo-born woman of extraordinary literary talent. She was described as 'brilliant in poetry composition, with excellent understanding and strength of spirit, unmatched in her qualities.' She married twice — first al-Nasiri Muhammad ibn al-Tunbugha, then Ala' Ali ibn Muhammad ibn Baybars (grandson of Sultan Barquq's sister). She performed hajj in 884 AH. Al-Sakhawi devoted 2,029 words to her — the longest women's entry in the entire dictionary.
notable: Longest women's entry in the entire Daw al-Lami'. Praised for intellectual brilliance and poetic composition.

name_arabic: أم هانئ بنت علي بن عبد الرحمن
name_english: Umm Hani bint Ali ibn Abd al-Rahman
father: Nur al-Din Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn al-Qadi Taqi al-Din
scholarly_activities: heard extensive hadith in Makkah at age 7
heard hadith in Cairo from multiple scholars
description_en: Umm Hani (also called Maryam) was born in Cairo in 778 AH to a distinguished scholarly family — granddaughter of Judge Fakhr al-Din al-Qayati. Her maternal grandfather took charge of her education, arranging for her to hear hadith in Makkah at age 7 from al-Nashawri and multiple other scholars, and in Cairo from another group. She became a significant hadith transmitter.
notable: Began formal hadith study at age 7 in Makkah. From a multi-generational scholarly family.

name_arabic: عائشة بنت علي بن محمد الكنانية
name_english: Aisha bint Ali ibn Muhammad al-Kinani
also_known_as: Called Sitt al-'Aysh (Lady of Life), also Umm Abdallah and Umm al-Fadl
teachers: her maternal grandfather Abu al-Haram (al-Fawa'id al-Ghaylaniyyat)
scholarly_activities: attended hadith sessions as a child
transmitted specific texts with documented chains
description_en: Aisha al-Kinani, called Sitt al-'Aysh, was a Hanbali woman from Cairo who began attending hadith sessions in childhood. She was presented before her maternal grandfather Abu al-Haram and heard five of eight sessions of al-Fawa'id al-Ghaylaniyyat, and studied with al-Izz ibn Jama'a and others. She transmitted texts with documented chains of authority.
notable: Hanbali woman — rare documentation of women in the Hanbali school. Multiple documented isnad chains.

name_arabic: رقية بنت الشرف محمد بن أبي الحسن
name_english: Ruqayya bint al-Sharaf Muhammad ibn Abi al-Hasan
father: al-Sharaf Muhammad, known as Ibn al-Qari'
family: From a hadith family. Her uncle Abu al-Faraj Abd al-Rahman was the musnad (highest authority) of Cairo. Wife of al-Qutb Abd al-Karim al-Halabi.
scholarly_activities: listed in her teacher's mu'jam (scholarly dictionary)
description_en: Ruqayya came from a distinguished hadith family of Damascus origin settled in Cairo. Her uncle was the musnad (highest hadith authority) of Cairo. She was mentioned in her teacher's mu'jam. Scholars sought her out and read to her, and her fame spread among hadith students. She granted ijaza to students — an act of scholarly certification.
notable: From a family where hadith scholarship was hereditary. Scholars came to HER to read — she was the authority, not the student.

also_known_as: Also called Aziza. Kunya: Umm al-Fadl
father: al-Muhaddith al-Sharaf Abu al-Fadl al-Qudsi al-Misri al-Shafi'i
scholarly_activities: became the highest isnad authority of her era
scholars crowded to study with her
description_en: Hajar (also called Aziza) was named after an older sister who died before her birth. Her father, a muhaddith, invested heavily in her education — presenting her and having her hear 'a great deal' of hadith collections, scholarly dictionaries, forty-hadith collections, and full books. By the end of her life, she became 'the highest isnad authority of her era' (asnad ahl asriha) and 'scholars crowded around her.'
notable: Described as 'the highest isnad authority of her era' — the most connected living link to the Prophet's words. Scholars crowded to study with her.

name_arabic: عائشة بنت محمد بن عبد الهادي المقدسية
name_english: Aisha bint Muhammad ibn Abd al-Hadi al-Maqdisiyya
also_known_as: Musnada al-Dunya (the world's highest hadith authority)
father: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Hadi ibn Qudama
family: Qurashi, Umari, Maqdisi — from the famous Ibn Qudama family
teachers: al-Sharaf Abdallah ibn al-Hasan (heard Sahih Muslim)
Abd al-Qadir ibn al-Muluk (heard Sirat Ibn Hisham)
description_en: Aisha al-Maqdisiyya was given the title 'Musnada al-Dunya' — literally 'the world's highest hadith authority.' From the famous Ibn Qudama scholarly dynasty of Damascus, she heard Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and Sirat Ibn Hisham from named teachers with documented chains. She received ijaza from scholars across the region including Sitt al-Fuqaha ('Lady of the Jurists'), another woman scholar.
notable: MUSNADA AL-DUNYA — 'the world's highest hadith authority.' A woman held the highest certification in hadith transmission in the entire Islamic world. She also received ijaza from another woman (Sitt al-Fuqaha).

name_arabic: زينب بنت الكمال محمد بن الناصر
name_english: Zaynab bint al-Kamal Muhammad ibn al-Nasir
family: Mother of al-Najm Yahya and Zubayda. Aunt of the Army Comptroller.
husband: Sultan al-Zahir Inal (married during his emirate, ~825 AH)
description_en: Zaynab married the future Sultan Inal during his time as an emir, around 825 AH. Remarkably, he never married another woman nor took a concubine — all his children were from her. Al-Sakhawi notes this was 'unique among all the kings.' She had extraordinary political influence: 'the Sultan was extremely obedient to her commands, to the point where he had no choice with her.' She performed hajj during her time of power, which was described as an extraordinary event.
notable: Wife of Sultan Inal who exercised real political power. The Sultan 'had no choice' against her commands — and he never married another. 'Unique among all the kings.'

name_arabic: عائشة بنت إبراهيم بن خليل الزبيدية
name_english: Aisha bint Ibrahim ibn Khalil al-Zubaydi
scholarly_activities: heard extensive hadith from companions of Fakhr ibn al-Bukhari in Damascus, Cairo, and Baalbek
description_en: Aisha al-Zubaydi was from a scholarly family — her brother was al-Jamal Abdallah, a hadith hafiz. Born in Damascus, she heard extensive hadith from multiple scholars across three cities: Damascus, Cairo, and Baalbek. Her teachers were companions of the great Fakhr ibn al-Bukhari. She represents the geographic mobility of female scholarship — travelling for knowledge across the Islamicate world.
notable: Studied across THREE cities (Damascus, Cairo, Baalbek). Sister of a hadith hafiz. Shows women's geographic mobility for scholarship.

name_arabic: خديجة بنت أمير حاج بن البيسري
name_english: Khadija bint Amir Hajj ibn al-Baysari
family: Descended from Badr al-Din, a famous commander. Granddaughter of a candidate for the sultanate.
description_en: Khadija came from a powerful military family — her ancestor was a famous commander who was even considered for the sultanate. She married four times: her first husband was killed in Damascus, the second died, the third was the superintendent of Jeddah (she performed hajj with him and stayed in Makkah), and the fourth also died while they were together. Despite the tragedies, she maintained her status and performed the pilgrimage.
notable: From a family of military commanders considered for the sultanate. Married four times — each husband died. Shows elite women's resilience and continued social standing.

name_arabic: زينب بنت الكمال محمد بن الناصر بن البارزي
name_english: Zaynab bint al-Kamal Muhammad ibn al-Nasir ibn al-Barizi
family: Mother of al-Najm Yahya and Zubayda. Aunt of the Army Comptroller (Nazir al-Jaysh). Her father married two sisters consecutively — she and her half-sister Fatima are both daughters and cousins.
description_en: Zaynab was from the prominent al-Barizi family of scholars and administrators. Her father al-Kamal married two sisters consecutively, making Zaynab and her half-sister Fatima both daughters and maternal cousins — al-Sakhawi notes this unusual genealogical detail. She died on a Friday before the prayer in Muharram 875 AH, having gone the previous day to visit her sister who was the wife of the hajj commander Yashbak al-Jamali.
notable: From the al-Barizi administrative dynasty. Illustrates the interconnected elite families of the Mamluk period.
